3: The notice that Ahaz ‘burned his son as an offering (wayyaʿăḇēr ʾet bᵉnô bāʾēš)’ — the heʿăḇîr bāʾēš formula (‘to cause to pass through the fire’) — is the OT’s technical expression for child sacrifice associated with the Mōleḵ cult. The formula appears throughout the Deuteronomistic literature (Deut 18:10; 2 Kgs 17:17; 21:6; 23:10; Jer 32:35) and the underlying practice is attested in the Hinnom valley (Gê Hinnom), where the Topheth installation was the site of the sacrifices. The Hebrew vocalization Mōleḵ has the consonants of melek (king) but the vowels of bōšet (shame) — the same scribal degradation applied to Baal-zebub and to Baal in personal names. Whether the formula describes actual child-burning or a rite of dedication-through-fire is debated (Mosca, Heider, Day), but the Deuteronomistic condemnation is consistent and unambiguous: this is the covenant-abomination of the nations, and Ahaz is the first Judahite king to practice it openly. Matt 25:41 (‘depart from me, you cursed, into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels’) and Rev 20:14-15 transpose the fire-of-sacrifice into the fire-of-judgment: the Gê Hinnom becomes Gehenna in the NT as the permanent destination of what the Topheth prefigured in miniature.
1: Ahaz son of Jotham begins his reign in the seventeenth year of Pekah. The synchronism places him during the height of the Syro-Ephraimite crisis that dominates vv5-9. Ahaz reigns sixteen years — a relatively short tenure for a Judean king, which the narrative implicitly connects to his faithlessness. His reign is the critical hinge point for the Isaiah narratives (Isa 7-8) and the book of Micah.
2: ben ʿeśrîm šānāh ʾāḥāz bᵉmamlᵉḵô — Ahaz was twenty years old when he began to reign. The regnal formula shifts immediately to the negative verdict: wᵉlōʾ ʿāśāh hayyāšār bᵉʿênê YHWH ʾĕlōhāyw kᵉdāwid ʾāḇîw — he did not do right in YHWH's eyes like David his father. The Davidic comparison, the standard benchmark for Judean kings, is withheld here — one of only a few Judean kings who fail even partial comparison. The contrast with Jotham (15:34) is stark.
4: wayzabbēaḥ wayzaqqēṭ babbāmôt ûʿal haggᵉḇāʿôt wᵉtaḥat kol-ʿēṣ raʿănān — he sacrificed and burned incense on the high places (bāmôt), on the hills, and under every green tree. The phrase taḥat kol-ʿēṣ raʿănān (under every lush/green tree) is formulaic Deuteronomistic language for pagan cultic groves (Deut 12:2; 2 Kgs 17:10; Jer 2:20; Hos 4:13). The three-location formula — high places, hills, green trees — describes a dispersed cultic system outside the Jerusalem temple that the DH consistently condemns as syncretistic.
5: The Syro-Ephraimite war: ʾāz yaʿăleh Rᵉṣîn melek ʾărām ûPeqaḥ ben-Rᵉmalyāhû melek yiśrāʾēl Yᵉrûšālaim lammilḥāmāh — then Rezin king of Aram and Pekah son of Remaliah came up against Jerusalem in war. The campaign is contemporaneous with Isaiah's Immanuel oracle (Isa 7:1-17). wayyāṣûrû ʿal ʾāḥāz wᵉlōʾ yāḵᵉlû lehillāḥēm — they besieged Ahaz but could not fight/prevail. The siege fails, setting the stage for Ahaz's appeal to Assyria rather than faith in YHWH (cf. Isa 7:4-9).
6: A parenthetical military note: Rezin king of Aram recovered Elath for Aram (hēšîḇ ʾet-ʾĒlat lăʾărām) and drove out the Judeans. The port of Elath on the Red Sea had been a significant Judean asset (cf. 14:22); its loss to Aram marks the contraction of Judean territory under Ahaz. waʾĂdōmîm bāʾû ʾĒlat wayyēšᵉḇû šām ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — the Edomites came to Elath and dwelt there to this day.
7: Ahaz's letter to Tiglath-Pileser king of Assyria: ʿaḇdᵉḵā ûḇinᵉḵā ʾānî — “I am your servant and your son.” This formula — ʿeḇed (servant) + bēn (son) — is the standard language of vassal submission in Mesopotamian diplomatic correspondence. Calling himself “son” to the Assyrian king is an extraordinary claim of subordination that inverts the normal covenant language in which YHWH is Israel's father and king. ʿălēh wᵉhôšîʿēnî miyyad melek ʾărām ûmiyyad melek yiśrāʾēl haqqāmîm ʿālay — “Come up and save me from the hand of the king of Aram and the king of Israel who rise against me.” Ahaz looks to Assyria rather than YHWH for salvation (hôšîʿēnî).
8: wayyiqqaḥ ʾāḥāz ʾet-hakkesep wᵉʾet-hazzāhāḇ hannimṣāʾ bêt YHWH ûbᵉʾôṣᵉrôt bêt hamméleḵ wayyišlaḥ lamélek ʾAššûr šōḥad — Ahaz took the silver and gold found in the house of YHWH and in the palace treasuries and sent it to the king of Assyria as a bribe (šōḥad). The term šōḥad (bribe, gift to obtain favor) is ethically loaded — it is elsewhere condemned in legal contexts (Deut 16:19; Exod 23:8). Taking temple treasure to pay a foreign king is a clear inversion of the proper order of tribute.
9: Tiglath-Pileser responds: wayyiqqaḥ melek ʾAššûr ʾet-Dammeśeq wayyiglēhā — the Assyrian king captured Damascus and exiled its population. wayyiglēhā is the qal of gālāh (to go into exile, to be deported) — the same root used in ch17 for Israel's exile. Damascus's fate prefigures what will happen to Samaria in ch17. wᵉʾet-Rᵉṣîn hēmît — and he killed Rezin. The Syro-Ephraimite threat is eliminated through Assyrian power that Ahaz purchased at the cost of temple treasure.
10: Ahaz travels to Damascus to meet Tiglath-Pileser — an act of political homage. There he sees (wayyarᵉ ʾāḥāz ʾet-hammizbēaḥ ʾăšer bᵉDammeśeq) the altar that was in Damascus. He sends to Uriah the priest the architectural specifications: dᵉmût hammizbēaḥ ûtabnîtô lᵉḵol-maʿăśēhû — the likeness (dᵉmût) of the altar and its pattern (tabnît) for all its workmanship. The use of tabnît (pattern, blueprint) recalls the tabernacle's heavenly blueprint (Exod 25:9) — here applied to a pagan altar model, a pointed irony.
11: Uriah the priest builds the altar: wayyiḇen ʾUriyyāh hakōhēn ʾet-hammizbēaḥ kᵉḵōl ʾăšer šālaḥ hammélek ʾāḥāz mēDammeśeq — according to all that King Ahaz sent from Damascus. Uriah is complicit — he carries out the king's instruction without prophetic objection recorded. The priest who enables royal apostasy is a recurring DH type. The speed of construction — completed before Ahaz returns — shows institutional compliance with royal innovation.
12: When the king returns from Damascus, he approaches (wayyiqrab hammélek ʾel hammizbēaḥ wayyaʿal ʿālāyw) the new altar and makes offerings on it. wayyaʿal — the technical term for ascending the altar (cf. Exod 20:26, which prohibits steps to the altar, though this is the Damascus model). The king himself performs the sacrificial acts, usurping a priestly function. The sequence of offerings — burnt, grain, libation, peace — mirrors the full sacrificial cycle.
13: wayyaqqēṭ ʾet-ʿōlātô wᵉʾet-minḥātô wayyisōḵ ʾet-niskô wayyizrōq ʾet-dam haššᵉlāmîm ʾăšer-lô ʿal hammizbēaḥ — he offered his burnt offering and grain offering, poured his drink offering and dashed the blood of his peace offerings on the altar. The term wayyaqqēṭ (hiphil of qāṭar, to cause smoke to rise as incense/offering) is different from ʿōlāh (whole burnt offering); here used for offering incense. The complete sacrificial sequence is performed on the foreign-modeled altar.
14: Ahaz orders the original bronze altar moved: wᵉʾet-hammizbāḥ hannᵉḥōšet ʾăšer lipnê YHWH wayyaqrēḇ mēʾet pᵉnê habbayt — the bronze altar that was before YHWH he removed from before the house. The bronze altar — probably the Solomonic altar (1 Kgs 8:64) — is displaced to the north, out of the primary sacrificial position. Ahaz repurposes it: wᵉhāyāh lî lᵉḇaqqēr — it shall be for me to inquire by (bāqar, to investigate/determine). The original altar becomes an oracular instrument while the Damascus-pattern altar becomes the primary sacrificial site.
15: Ahaz's orders to Uriah: ʿal hammizbēaḥ haggādôl qṭr ʾet-ʿōlat habboqer wᵉʾet minḥat hāʿereḇ — on the great altar burn the morning burnt offering and the evening grain offering. The liturgical schedule — morning burnt offering, evening grain offering, royal offering — was the Temple's daily rhythm. Ahaz transfers all official worship to the new altar. The original Solomonic altar is reserved for his personal divination (yihyeh lî lᵉḇaqqēr). This is a systematic liturgical replacement, not mere syncretism.
16: wayyaʿaś ʾUriyyāh hakōhēn kᵉḵōl ʾăšer ṣiwwāh hammélek ʾāḥāz — Uriah the priest did according to all that King Ahaz commanded. The repetition of the priest's compliance underscores the institutional failure: the Jerusalem priesthood executed royal liturgical innovation without prophetic resistance. The comparison with Moses' priestly obedience to YHWH's commands in Exodus is implicit and damning — here a priest obeys a king rather than YHWH.
17: Further dismantling of the temple furnishings: Ahaz cuts the frames (wayyᵉqaṣṣēṣ ʾāḥāz ʾet-hammisgᵉrôt) of the bronze stands and removes the basins. He also removes the Bronze Sea from the bronze oxen supporting it and sets it on a stone pavement. The Bronze Sea and its twelve ox supports were among the signature Solomonic furnishings (1 Kgs 7:23-26); stripping them signals both theological deconstruction and political-economic urgency — tribute to Assyria requires precious metal.
18: The covered way (mûsak haššabbāt) built in the temple is removed, and the royal entrance (ûmebôʾ hammélek haḥîṣônāh) is turned toward the Assyrian king's requirements. The term mûsak haššabbāt (the Sabbath covering, or Sabbath portico) is unique to this verse; it may have been a structure built for special Sabbath royal participation in worship. Its removal “on account of the king of Assyria” suggests it was either too ostentatiously Israelite or was dismantled for its materials. The verse completes the picture: Ahaz has subordinated the entire temple apparatus to Assyrian vassalage.
19: The standard closing formula: wᵉyeter diḇrê ʾāḥāz ʾăšer ʿāśāh hălōʾ hēm kᵉtûḇîm ʿal sēper diḇrê hayyāmîm lᵉmalkê yᵉhûdāh — the rest of the acts of Ahaz, are they not written in the Book of Chronicles of the Kings of Judah? The formula signals the official court record as source material. Ahaz's full chronicle — including alliances, building projects, and presumably the full altar correspondence — was preserved in the annals now lost.
20: wayyiškaḇ ʾāḥāz ʿim ʾăḇōtāyw wayyiqqāḇēr ʿim ʾăḇōtāyw bᵉʿîr dāwid — Ahaz slept with his fathers and was buried with his fathers in the city of David. The burial notation carries no special honor — he is buried in David's city but there is no note of burial in a specific tomb of kings (contrast Hezekiah's honored burial, 2 Chr 32:33). wayyimloḵ ḥizqiyyāhû bᵉnô taḥtāyw — his son Hezekiah reigned in his place. The contrast between Ahaz (most apostate Judean king to this point) and Hezekiah (one of the most faithful) is the sharpest royal reversal in Kings.
